skill.name: etetoolkit
skill.description: Use ETE Toolkit for phylogenetic tree manipulation, taxonomy integration, evolutionary event analysis, and publication-ready tree visualization.
skill.summary: Analyze, transform, compare, and visualize phylogenetic trees with ETE Toolkit guidance and helper scripts.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found the Ruby/backtick and weak-cryptography alerts are false positives from markdown code fences and phylogenetics terminology. Real risks remain moderate: NCBI taxonomy can download and cache data, documentation recommends sudo package installation, and helper scripts write to user-supplied paths.

medium_findings.title: NCBI taxonomy download and hidden local cache
medium_findings.description: The skill documents NCBITaxa behavior that downloads about 300 MB and stores a taxonomy database under the user home directory. This is legitimate ETE behavior, but it creates network and local data-cache implications that users should know before use.
medium_findings.recommendation: Warn users before NCBI taxonomy initialization or updates, and prefer explicit project documentation for cache location and network requirements.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly states the automatic download and hidden cache path. The behavior is expected for ETE taxonomy support, so the concern is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Privileged package installation guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes sudo apt-get commands for optional Qt rendering dependencies. These commands are documentation examples, not automatically executed code, but copied commands would run with elevated privileges.
medium_findings.recommendation: Keep sudo commands clearly labeled as optional system dependency installation and advise users to review them before running.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo command appears in fenced installation and troubleshooting sections. No code path invokes it automatically, but the privilege boundary is real if a user follows the example.

low_findings.title: Helper scripts write to user-selected output paths
low_findings.description: The scripts render or write tree outputs to paths supplied on the command line. This is normal CLI behavior, but the scripts do not check whether those paths already exist before writing.
low_findings.recommendation: Advise users to choose explicit output directories and avoid pointing outputs at important existing files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write locations are direct and user controlled. This supports the skill purpose and does not show unauthorized filesystem access.

low_findings.title: External command static hits are markdown examples
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts come from fenced Markdown examples that show Python, uv, brew, and apt commands. They are not executed by the skill unless a user manually runs them.
low_findings.recommendation: Treat these as documentation examples and keep commands scoped to the stated ETE workflow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is in Markdown code fences or examples. I found no subprocess, os.system, or shell execution call in the scripts.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography alerts are false positives
low_findings.description: The weak-algorithm static findings map to ordinary ETE terms and examples such as node support, colors, and tree traversal. No cryptographic code or hash-selection logic was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.recommendation: Dismiss these static hits unless future versions add actual cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited contexts are visualization labels and tree statistics. They do not implement encryption, hashing, or credential handling.

low_findings.title: Optional external platform promotion in skill instructions
low_findings.description: The skill asks the assistant to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is not a direct security threat, but it is behavior steering toward an external service and should be reviewed as marketplace content.
low_findings.recommendation: Review whether promotional guidance is acceptable for community marketplace listings and ensure it remains optional.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text is explicit promotional guidance, but it does not tell the model to bypass security review, ignore instructions, or exfiltrate data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network-backed taxonomy initialization
dangerous_patterns.description: NCBITaxa setup and updates may contact NCBI and create or refresh a local taxonomy database.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Require user awareness before using taxonomy setup or update workflows.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented directly and is part of ETE functionality. It is not hidden or malicious, but it uses network and local storage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged installation command examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes sudo apt-get commands for optional rendering dependencies.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Keep privileged commands optional and clearly separated from normal skill use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in install sections and are not automatically executed. The risk comes from user copy-paste with elevated privileges.

dangerous_patterns.title: Filesystem writes from helper scripts
dangerous_patterns.description: Helper scripts write converted, rerooted, pruned, or rendered outputs to command-line paths.
dangerous_patterns.recommendation: Use explicit output paths and avoid overwriting source files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The write behavior is straightforward CLI output handling. I found no attempt to target sensitive paths automatically.

content.user_title: Analyze phylogenetic trees with ETE Toolkit
content.value_statement: Phylogenetic tree workflows often need format conversion, taxonomy lookup, visualization, and orthology analysis. This skill provides ETE patterns and helper scripts for reproducible tree analysis.
actual_capabilities: Explains ETE Tree, PhyloTree, ClusterTree, and NCBITaxa workflows with practical examples.
Provides helper scripts for tree statistics, format conversion, rerooting, pruning, leaf listing, and ASCII output.
Guides evolutionary event detection, orthology and paralogy review, and species naming configuration.
Covers NCBI taxonomy translation, lineage retrieval, topology generation, and tree annotation.
Supports publication-oriented tree rendering patterns for PDF, SVG, and PNG outputs.
Documents tree comparison, Robinson-Foulds distance, clustering dendrograms, and heatmap-style visualizations.
limitations: ETE and optional GUI dependencies must be installed outside the skill.
NCBI taxonomy features may download and cache a large local database on first use.
Rendering depends on Qt and may require system packages on some platforms.
The helper scripts do not protect against overwriting user-selected output files.

use_cases.title: Prepare trees for analysis
use_cases.description: Convert Newick formats, reroot trees, prune taxa, and summarize tree structure before downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Review gene family evolution
use_cases.description: Use PhyloTree workflows to detect duplication and speciation events and separate orthologs from paralogs.

use_cases.title: Create publication figures
use_cases.description: Apply ETE styling patterns to produce labeled, supported, and editable tree figures for manuscripts.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ETE Toolkit skill to inspect my Newick tree file. Report leaf count, total nodes, branch length statistics, and any obvious parsing assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a filtered tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me reroot this tree at the midpoint, prune it to a provided taxa list, preserve branch lengths, and save a clean Newick output.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze evolutionary events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use PhyloTree guidance to configure species extraction, detect speciation and duplication events, and summarize likely ortholog and paralog groups.

prompt_templates.title: Build a taxonomy-aware figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an ETE workflow that annotates leaves with NCBI taxonomy, highlights selected clades, displays support values, and renders PDF and SVG figures.

output_examples.input: User asks for a quick review of a Newick tree.
output_examples.output: A concise tree statistics report with leaf count, node count, depth, branch length summary, and suggested format settings.

output_examples.input: User provides a gene tree and species naming rule.
output_examples.output: A workflow summary that identifies evolutionary events, separates ortholog candidates, and lists assumptions about species labels.

output_examples.input: User requests a manuscript-ready tree figure.
output_examples.output: A rendering plan with layout mode, support-value styling, label handling, output formats, and dependency notes.

best_practices: Validate the Newick format number before running conversions or downstream analysis.
Use iterator-based traversal for large trees to reduce memory pressure.
Keep taxonomy downloads, rendered figures, and processed trees in explicit project directories.
anti_patterns: Do not run taxonomy updates when network access or large local caches are not acceptable.
Do not overwrite original trees without keeping an unchanged source copy.
Do not assume leaf names encode species correctly without checking the naming rule.

faq.question: Does this skill install ETE automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides commands and examples, but users must install ETE and optional rendering dependencies themselves.

faq.question: Can it work with Newick and NHX trees?
faq.answer: Yes. The documentation covers Newick format options and NHX feature preservation.

faq.question: Does it need internet access?
faq.answer: Only NCBI taxonomy setup or updates need internet access. Basic tree operations can run offline.

faq.question: Where does NCBI taxonomy data go?
faq.answer: ETE may cache the taxonomy database under the user home directory in the ETE toolkit folder.

faq.question: Can it create publication graphics?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes rendering patterns for PDF, SVG, and PNG, subject to local Qt dependencies.

faq.question: Is this suitable for very large trees?
faq.answer: Yes, with care. The skill recommends iterator-based traversal and cached content for large tree workflows.
